title-group.article-title: Risk factors of venous thromboembolism in ICU patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: Department of Critical Care Medicine, Zhejiang Hospital, Hangzhou, China

fn.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/07853890.2026.2614222.

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Yuehong Qiu 13605814020@163.com Department of Critical Care Medicine, Zhejiang Hospital, Lingyin Road 12, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013 China.

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This study aimed to identify risk factors associated with the development of VTE in patients admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic literature search was conducted via PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Cochrane databases up to 25 April 2025, to identify studies examining the association between risk factors and the occurrence of venous thromboembolism (VTE) in ICU patients. Data were pooled using odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 2465 relevant studies were identified through the systematic search, of which 30 were included in the meta-analysis. The pooled data showed that the following were significant risk factors for venous thromboembolism (VTE) in ICU patients: central venous catheterization (OR = 2.67, 95% CI: 1.67–4.28; I2 = 28%), invasive mechanical ventilation (OR = 2.08, 95% CI: 1.46–2.96; I2 = 0%), advanced age (OR = 2.06, 95% CI: 1.28–3.31; I2 = 86%), length of ICU stay (OR = 4.24, 95% CI: 1.43–12.57; I2 = 98%), malignancy (OR = 2.30, 95% CI: 1.03–5.12; I2 = 67%), elevated D-dimer levels (OR = 2.46, 95% CI: 1.37–4.40; I2 = 34%), and a history of VTE (OR = 2.84, 95% CI: 1.45–5.55; I2 = 51%). According to the GRADE assessment, the quality of evidence was rated as moderate for invasive mechanical ventilation, low for central venous catheterization and D-dimer levels, and very low for the remaining factors.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Invasive mechanical ventilation, central venous catheterization, and elevated D-dimer levels are associated with VTE risk, supported by relatively high-quality evidence. These findings may help identify ICU patients at higher risk of VTE, inform the development of risk assessment models for patient stratification, and ultimately contribute to improved prognosis through optimal screening and management strategies.

funding-group.funding-statement: The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) refers to the abnormal coagulation of blood within the venous system, which can partially or completely obstruct the vessel, leading to deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary thromboembolism (PTE) [1]. These represent two different clinical stages and manifestations of VTE and together constitute a common global health problem. As the third leading cause of cardiovascular-related death worldwide [2], VTE ranks only behind coronary artery disease and ischemic stroke [3]. Epidemiological studies show that the annual incidence of VTE in the United States is approximately 310 per 100,000 population, while in Europe, the rate has increased from 104 to 183 per 100,000 [4]. In China, the prevalence reached 127.8 per 100,000 in 2021, and the incidence continues to rise annually. If left untreated, VTE can have a mortality rate as high as 25%, although this can be reduced to 1–5% with appropriate treatment, posing a serious threat to patient health.

Among ICU patients, multiple underlying diseases, prolonged bed rest, and reduced mobility significantly increase the risk of VTE. This condition can interrupt ongoing treatment, lead to disease recurrence, and cause serious complications such as venous stenosis and pulmonary embolism, thereby greatly endangering patient safety [5].

Previous studies have shown that advanced age, prolonged bed rest, and a prior history of thrombosis significantly increase the risk of VTE in ICU patients. In a 2024 study on the development of a VTE risk assessment tool for hospitalized ICU patients, Chuanlin Zhan [6] included 566 individuals and, after four years of observation, identified platelet count in the ICU, invasive mechanical ventilation, and prolonged immobility as significant risk factors for VTE.

Similarly, Zhihong Tang, in a 2025 study using a nomogram to predict lower limb deep vein thrombosis in elderly ICU patients following severe traumatic brain injury surgery [7], analyzed 401 patients over a seven-year period and found that D-dimer levels and longer surgical duration were key risk factors. To date, the exact risk factors for VTE in ICU patients remain inconclusive.

In 2021, Hervé Lobbes et al. published a meta-analysis including 21 studies [8][(published between 1 January 2020 and 15 April 2021), which found that in severe COVID-19 patients, the risk factors for venous thromboembolism included correlations between peak D-dimer levels, length of hospital stay, and intubation. Their study also identified low-certainty evidence associating VTE with C-reactive protein (CRP), D-dimer, troponin T, and vasopressor requirement. However, this research focused exclusively on COVID-19 patients and specifically on VTE risk in severe COVID-19 cases.

Since the publication of Hervé Lobbes’ study, numerous new studies have emerged [6,7,9–12], and their conclusions are not entirely consistent with the previous findings. Therefore, the aim of the present study is to analyze, on a broader scale, all potential risk factors associated with VTE in ICU patients, in order to provide the most up-to-date and comprehensive evidence-based data. This will further verify which factors may increase the risk of VTE in ICU patients and guide clinicians in implementing proactive and effective preventive measures to reduce VTE incidence and improve ICU patient outcomes.

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p: This study was conducted in accordance with the guidelines outlined in the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses 2020 (PRISMA 2020) statement (18) [13]. The research protocol was registered with the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO: CRD420251075077). Two researchers (XLY and XLZ) were responsible for developing the search strategy and collaboratively created the MeSH terms and keywords for retrieving relevant studies from multiple databases, including PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library. The literature search covered all publications from the inception of the databases until 25 April 2025. The search terms used were: ‘ICU’, ‘venous thrombosis’, and ‘risk factors’. Supplemental Table S1 presents the results of the literature search strategy. Two researchers independently screened the titles and abstracts of studies retrieved from the databases, downloaded the full-text articles, and assessed them for eligibility. Any discrepancies arising during the study selection process were resolved through consensus.

sec.title: Inclusion criteria
sec.p: P (Population): Patients admitted to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for various reasons, primarily including respiratory diseases, cardiovascular diseases, and surgical trauma.

I (Intervention/Exposure): Specific risk factors associated with the patients, including demographic characteristics (e.g. advanced age, sex), underlying diseases (e.g. malignant tumors, history of venous thromboembolism (VTE)), interventions (e.g. central venous catheterization, invasive mechanical ventilation), laboratory indicators (e.g. D-dimer levels), and prolonged ICU stay.
C (Comparison): In cohort studies, ICU patients without the aforementioned specific risk factors; in case-control studies, ICU patients who have not experienced VTE.

O (Outcome): The occurrence of venous thromboembolism (VTE) during the ICU stay, diagnosed through imaging tests (e.g. Doppler ultrasound confirming deep vein thrombosis, or CT pulmonary angiography confirming pulmonary embolism) or clinical assessment.
S (Study Type): Cohort studies or case-control studies.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Study protocols, unpublished studies, non-original research (including letters, reviews, abstracts, errata, and replies), animal studies, in vitro experiments, studies lacking sufficient data, and reviews were excluded.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Two investigators (XLY and XLZ) independently extracted the data. Discrepancies were resolved by a third author (YHQ). The following information was extracted from eligible studies: first author’s name, publication year, country (study location), study design, study period, study population, sample size, patient sex, patient age, BMI, and risk factors. For continuous variables (e.g. age, BMI), if the original study has categorized them (e.g. defining old age as 65 years and above), we directly adopt their definitions. If the data is continuous, we retain the original values for analysis. For categorical variables (e.g. sex, medical history), we use a binary coding system (e.g. ‘Yes’ assigned a value of 1, ‘No’ or ‘None’ assigned a value of 0) to ensure data consistency. Regarding missing data, we first contact the corresponding author via email to attempt to obtain the missing information. For data that cannot be supplemented, we explicitly report the missing data proportions for each study in the manuscript and only combine data that has been reported in the statistical analysis to avoid introducing bias.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The Newcastle–Ottawa Scale (NOS) [14] was used to assess the quality of the studies included in our meta-analysis. The evaluation was based on three parameters: selection, comparability, and outcome, with a maximum score of 9 points for each study. Studies scoring between 7 and 9 points were classified as high-quality. Two authors (XLY and XLZ) reviewed the titles and abstracts of studies retrieved from the databases, downloaded the full texts, and assessed their eligibility.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Pooled odds ratios (ORs) and their 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated to assess risk factors for venous thromboembolism (VTE) in ICU patients. Heterogeneity was evaluated using Cochrane’s Q test and Higgins’ I2 statistic. An I2 value greater than 50% or a p-value less than 0.1 indicated significant heterogeneity. A random-effects model was applied for all analyses. Additionally, sensitivity analyses were conducted to explore the stability of the results and potential sources of heterogeneity. Publication bias was assessed using Egger’s test. A p-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. For results with publication bias, the impact of publication bias on the results was assessed using the trim-and-fill method.All statistical analyses were performed using STATA 18.0 and Review Manager 5.4 software. Furthermore, according to GRADE, the quality of evidence for each outcome was evaluated and classified as ‘high’, ‘moderate’, ‘low’, or ‘very low’ for final evidence recommendations. As shown in the graphical abstract (Supplemental Figure S1), the complete logical chain from ‘Research Methods’ to ‘Key Findings’, and ultimately to ‘Clinical Significance and Future Perspectives’, is summarized in a flowchart format. Key risk factors and their corresponding levels of evidence are clearly indicated, with a particular emphasis on the conclusion that invasive mechanical ventilation, central venous catheterization, and elevated D-dimer are important factors for guiding VTE risk stratification and prevention in ICU patients.

sec.title: Literature search, study characteristics, and baseline data
sec.p: The flowchart of the literature search and selection process is shown in Figure 1. A total of 2,465 articles were initially retrieved from the database search. Among them, 811 articles were excluded due to duplication. After screening the titles and abstracts of the remaining studies, 1,540 studies were excluded. Subsequently, a total of 1,076 irrelevant studies were excluded, along with 217 conference abstracts, book chapters, letters, etc.; 52 non-English studies; and 195 meta-analyses and systematic reviews.Full texts of 114 studies were assessed for eligibility. Of these, 56 studies were excluded for irrelevance, and 28 studies were excluded mainly due to the lack of relevant data necessary for analyzing risk factors of venous thromboembolism (VTE). Ultimately, 30 studies [1,6,7,9–12,15–37] were included in this meta-analysis, as shown in Table 1. Among the 30 eligible studies, one study [36] was conducted in Japan, four studies [25–28] in Canada, five studies [12,24,29,31,35] in the United States, eight studies [6,7,10,23,30,32,33,37] in China, and the remaining studies were conducted in Europe. Notably, 21 studies were retrospective, while 9 were prospective. All studies were published in English between 2000 and 2025. The total sample size of the studies ranged from a minimum of 76 to a maximum of 1,465,020 participants. The number of male participants ranged from 49 to 831,050, and the number of female participants ranged from 46 to 6,334,444. The average age across all studies ranged from 45.3 to 76.3 years. The average BMI ranged from 21.97 kg/m2 to 31.1 kg/m2. Table 1lists the characteristics of the included studies. All 30 studies scored between 6 and 9 on the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS), indicating high quality (see Supplemental Table S2, Table S3).

caption.p: Basic characteristics of the included literature.

Patients in intensive care units (icus) in Australia and New Zealand
Patients admitted to the intensive care unit due to internal (non-surgical) diseases

sec.title: Interventional risk factors
sec.p: Invasive mechanical ventilation was included in 5 studies [1,6,10,25,29]. Meta-analysis showed that invasive mechanical ventilation significantly increased the risk of VTE in ICU patients (OR = 2.08, 95% CI: 1.46–2.96; I2 = 0%) (Figure 2(A)). Central venous catheterization was included in 5 studies [6,9,26,29,37]. Meta-analysis revealed that central venous catheterization significantly increased the risk of VTE in ICU patients (OR = 2.67, 95% CI: 1.67–4.28; I2 = 28%) (Figure 2(B)).

caption.p: Forest plot-Interventional Risk Factors (A): Central venous catheter; (B): Invasive mechanical ventilation.

sec.title: Non-interventional risk factors
sec.p: Age was included in 7 studies [6,7,9,10,23,32,36]. Meta-analysis demonstrated a significant association between age and the incidence of VTE, with higher age linked to an increased risk of VTE (OR = 2.06, 95% CI: 1.28–3.31; I2= 86%) (Figure 3(A)).

caption.p: Forest plot-Non-Interventional Risk Factors (A): Age; (B): Length of stay in the ICU; (C): Malignancy; (D): D-dimer; (E): History of venous thromboembolis.

sec.p: Length of ICU stay was reported in 3 studies [1,23,37]. Meta-analysis showed that longer ICU stay was significantly associated with a higher risk of VTE (OR = 4.24, 95% CI: 1.43–12.57; I2 = 98%) (Figure 3(B)).

Malignant tumors were analyzed in 4 studies [10,20,23,31]. Meta-analysis indicated that malignancy significantly increased the risk of VTE in ICU patients (OR = 2.30, 95% CI: 1.03–5.12; I2 = 67%) (Figure 3(C)).

D- dimer levels were included in 3 studies [7,32,33]. Meta-analysis found a significant correlation between elevated D-dimer and VTE incidence, with higher D-dimer levels associated with increased VTE risk (OR = 2.46, 95% CI: 1.37–4.40; I2 = 34%) (Figure 3(D)).

History of VTE was reported in 6 studies [15,18,26,28,31,35]. Meta-analysis showed that a prior history of VTE significantly increased the risk of VTE in ICU patients (OR = 2.84, 95% CI: 1.45–5.55; I2= 51%) (Figure 3(E)).

sec.title: Risk factors without statistical significance
sec.p: Our analysis found no significant association between the following risk factors and the risk of VTE in ICU patients: renal failure (OR = 1.03, 95% CI: 0.79–1.34; I2 = 68%)(Supplemental Figure S2); nephrotic syndrome (OR = 1.18, 95% CI: 0.83–1.69; I2 = 0%) (Supplemental Figure S3); SOFA score (OR = 1.01, 95% CI: 0.89–1.15; I2 = 59% (Supplemental Figure S4); BMI (OR = 1.26, 95% CI: 0.75–2.13; I2 = 99% (Supplemental Figure S5); ICU platelet count (OR = 1.27, 95% CI: 0.78–2.07; I2 = 75%) (Supplemental Figure S6); respiratory failure (OR = 0.93, 95% CI: 0.64–1.36; I2 = 0%) (Supplemental Figure S7); sepsis (OR = o.75, 95% CI: 0.42–1.35; I2 = 37%) (Supplemental Figure S8); Caprini score (OR = 1.70, 95% CI: 0.74–3.92; I2 = 79%) (Supplemental Figure S9); APACHE II score(OR = 1.02, 95% CI: 0.99–1.06; I2 = 62% (Supplemental Figure S10); SAPS II score > 41(OR = 0.71, 95% CI: 0.28–1.81; I2 = 64%) (Supplemental Figure S11); prolonged immobility (OR = 2.58 95% CI: 0.49–13.59; I2 = 78%) (Supplemental Figure S12); male sex (OR = 1.11, 95% CI: 0.85–1.44; I2 = 70%) (Supplemental Figure S13); blood transfusion (OR = 1.97, 95% CI: 0.92–4.22; I2 = 69%) (Supplemental Figure S14); duration of surgery (OR = 1.01, 95% CI: 0.10–10.39; I2 = 93%) (Supplemental Figure S15); new onset atrial fibrillation (OR = 1.17, 95% CI: 0.66–2.08; I2 = 0%) (Supplemental Figure S16); major bleeding (OR = 1.44, 95% CI: 0.76–2.73; I2 = 0%) (Supplemental Figure S17); minor bleeding (OR = 1.81, 95% CI: 0.63–5.20; I2 = 58%) (Supplemental Figure S18); cardiovascular disease (OR = 1.31, 95% CI: 0.50–3.47; I2 = 73%) (Supplemental Figure S19). These factors were not significantly correlated with the risk of VTE in ICU patients.

sec.title: Sensitivity analysis
sec.p: In our study, we conducted a sensitivity analysis by sequentially excluding each eligible study to assess the robustness of the results regarding the clinical significance of risk factors associated with the occurrence of VTE in ICU patients. The results showed that for the following factors – invasive mechanical ventilation (Supplemental Figure S20), D-dimer (Supplemental Figure S21), and history of venous thromboembolism (Supplemental Figure S22), central venous catheter (Supplemental Figure S23), age (Supplemental Figure S24), length of stay in the ICU (Supplemental Figure S25) – the effect sizes remained consistent within the original range after the exclusion of each individual study. This indicates that no single study had a disproportionate impact on the results for these risk factors, thus confirming the reliability of the analysis.

Moreover, our sensitivity analysis suggests that the results for renal failure (Supplemental Figure S26), SOFA score (Supplemental Figure S27), BMI (Supplemental Figure S28), malignancy (Supplemental Figure S29), platelet count upon ICU admission (Supplemental Figure S30), sepsis (Supplemental Figure S31), APACHE II score (Supplemental Figure S32), prolonged immobility (Supplemental Figure S33), and massive transfusion (Supplemental Figure S34) were unstable. Currently, there is insufficient evidence to confirm a significant association between these risk factors and the occurrence of VTE in ICU patients.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Egger’s test was used to assess publication bias. The results of Egger’s test revealed significant publication bias in the meta-analyses of the following risk factors: central venous catheter (Egger’s test: p = 0.033), age (p = 0.000), BMI (p = 0.017), length of stay in the ICU (p = 0.009), and massive transfusion (p = 0.000). The impact of publication bias on the significance of central venous catheter use, age, BMI, length of ICU stay, and massive transfusion was assessed using the trim-and-fill method. The results indicated that after trimming and filling, the significance of central venous catheter use (Figure S35), age (Supplemental Figure S36), BMI (Supplemental Figure S37), length of ICU stay (Supplemental Figure S38), and massive transfusion (Supplemental Figure S39) remained unchanged, suggesting that publication bias did not have a significant impact on these factors.

In contrast, the Egger’s test indicated no significant publication bias for the following variables: renal failure (p = 0.752), SOFA score (p = 0.595), malignancy (p = 0.920), platelet count upon ICU admission (p = 0.515), invasive mechanical ventilation (p = 0.418), D-dimer (p = 0.463), sepsis (p = 0.353), APACHE II score (p = 0.720), history of venous thromboembolism (p = 0.757), and prolonged immobility (p = 0.750).

sec.title: GRADE assessment
sec.p: According to the GRADE assessment, invasive mechanical ventilation was rated as moderate-quality evidence. Central venous catheter and D-dimer were rated as low-quality evidence. The following variables were rated as very low-quality evidence: renal failure, nephrotic syndrome, SOFA score, age, BMI, length of stay in the ICU, malignancy, platelet count upon ICU admission, respiratory failure, sepsis, and Caprini score.Common downgrading factors include heterogeneity, publication bias, and imprecision. Detailed GRADE ratings are presented in Table 2.

tr.td: Length of stay in the ICU

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our study identified central venous catheterization, invasive mechanical ventilation, age, length of ICU stay, malignancy, D-dimer levels, and history of VTE as significant risk factors for the development of VTE in ICU patients. Notably, sensitivity analyses revealed that the results for renal failure, SOFA score, BMI, malignancy, platelet count upon ICU admission, sepsis, APACHE II score, prolonged immobility, and massive transfusion were unstable. Moreover, Egger’s test indicated publication bias for central venous catheter, age, BMI, length of ICU stay, and massive transfusion, which contributed to the downgrading of the quality of evidence. To provide the most objective evidence-based recommendations, we applied the GRADE approach to comprehensively assess these factors that may impact evidence quality. The GRADE assessment revealed that invasive mechanical ventilation was supported by moderate-quality evidence, while central venous catheterization and D-dimer were supported by low-quality evidence. All other factors were rated as very low-quality. Therefore, in ICU settings, special attention should be paid to the timing of initiating invasive mechanical ventilation and central venous catheterization, as well as monitoring dynamic changes in D-dimer levels, to identify high-risk patients early and minimize the incidence of VTE.

Several risk factors identified in this study (e.g. advanced age, prolonged ICU stay, malignant tumors) showed significant statistical associations, but their pooled results exhibited high heterogeneity (e.g. length of ICU stay, I2 = 98%). We analyzed that this variability mainly stemmed from multiple differences between the included studies: clinical heterogeneity in patient baseline characteristics (such as disease severity, admission diagnosis, and comorbidities) [38], inconsistencies in thromboprophylaxis strategies across centers (including drug selection, timing, and adherence) [39,40], as well as differences in VTE diagnostic criteria (routine screening vs. clinical suspicion) and monitoring frequency [41,42]. Although high heterogeneity reduces the precision of the effect size and lowers the level of evidence, these factors still hold significant clinical warning value. They should not be used as independent decision-making criteria, but rather integrated as key elements in comprehensive risk assessments. We recommend heightened vigilance for patients with such characteristics (e.g. elderly individuals with malignant tumors) [43], considering them as a priority group for VTE prevention. This may include dynamic monitoring of D-dimer levels and the use of tools like the Caprini score for stratified management, in order to optimize resource allocation and implement individualized early prevention in situations where evidence is still incomplete.

Our findings are consistent with those of Hervé Lobbes, who conducted a meta-analysis of 5296 ICU patients across 21 observational studies [8], and found that length of hospital stay and D-dimer levels influenced the risk of VTE. In addition, our study newly included a broader range of potential risk factors, such as central venous catheterization, invasive mechanical ventilation, age, malignancy, history of VTE, renal failure, SOFA score, BMI, platelet count upon ICU admission, sepsis, APACHE II score, prolonged immobility, and massive transfusion. We further verified the quality of evidence through sensitivity analysis and GRADE assessment. Our analysis confirmed that central venous catheterization, invasive mechanical ventilation, age, malignancy, and history of VTE were significantly associated with an increased risk of VTE, whereas renal failure, SOFA score, BMI, malignancy, platelet count upon ICU admission, sepsis, APACHE II score, and prolonged immobility were not significantly associated with VTE occurrence. The discrepancies between our findings and previous studies may be attributed to our larger sample size, more diverse patient population, and updated literature, all of which support the robustness and relevance of our results based on the current quality of evidence.

Invasive mechanical ventilation, as a commonly used intervention in ICU patients, was identified in our study as an independent and significant risk factor for the development of VTE, with an odds ratio (OR) of 2.08. In critically ill patients, invasive procedures related to mechanical ventilation can damage the integrity of the vascular endothelium. Additionally, these patients often remain bedridden for prolonged periods, leading to reduced activity of the calf muscle pump. Mechanical ventilation also alters intrathoracic pressure, slows venous return, and promotes blood stasis, increasing the likelihood of thrombus formation. Furthermore, invasive ventilation may induce immobility, a hypercoagulable state, and a stress response that elevates coagulation factors while reducing anticoagulant levels. Inflammatory mediators also activate platelets, all of which contribute to an elevated risk of VTE [44,45]. Based on current evidence and guideline recommendations, the initiation of invasive mechanical ventilation should follow strict clinical criteria [46,47], such as: respiratory failure indicators: respiratory rate >35 breaths/min (persisting >2 h despite adequate oxygen therapy), or PaO2/FiO2 <150 mmHg (after excluding cardiac causes) [48]; Disrupted physiological compensation: pH <7.25 or PaCO2 >60 mmHg with altered mental status (e.g. GCS ≤12), indicating respiratory muscle fatigue or central dysfunction [49]; early signs of multiple organ failure: including shock or severe metabolic acidosis (e.g. lactate >4 mmol/L). Even in cases where invasive ventilation is required, pharmacological prophylaxis (e.g. low molecular weight heparin) and mechanical prophylaxis (e.g. compression stockings) should be initiated within 24 h of intubation, provided there are no contraindications [50]. For immunocompromised or elderly patients, a more cautious approach is warranted, weighing the risks and benefits of intubation. It is recommended to establish a multidisciplinary decision-making process and adopt the ‘ABCDE bundle strategy’ (Awakening, Breathing coordination, Choice of sedation, Delirium monitoring, Early mobility) to systematically assess the necessity of mechanical ventilation [51], thereby avoiding premature interventions based solely on clinical intuition. In addition, daily reassessment of indications is essential. Accurate documentation of ventilation parameters, prompt response to alarms, and efforts to reduce the duration of mechanical ventilation are critical measures to minimize the risk of VTE.

In this study, central venous catheterization was identified as an important risk factor for ICU-acquired venous thromboembolism (VTE). During catheterization, endothelial injury may occur, and after catheter placement, patient mobility is limited, resulting in slowed blood flow and increased risk of thrombosis [52,53]. Catheters may also induce local infection, with inflammatory stimuli further promoting thrombus formation [54]. Particularly for femoral vein catheterization, studies show that the incidence of catheter-related thrombosis ranges from 2.2% to 69%, whereas subclavian vein catheter-related thrombosis incidence ranges from 2% to 10%. The risk of catheter-related thrombosis increases with prolonged catheter dwell time [55]. To avoid overtreatment, catheters should be used only when necessary (e.g. for long-term parenteral nutrition, central venous pressure monitoring, or chemotherapy) and should be avoided for short-term intravenous access needs (≤7 days). Research indicates that catheter dwell time longer than 7 days increases the risk of VTE by 2.3 times. In emergency rescue situations, peripheral large vein access should be prioritized [56]. For short-term treatment (<72 h) or infusion of non-irritant drugs, peripheral venous catheters or midline catheters are preferred [57]. For patients requiring blood purification, the possibility of using temporary dialysis catheters should be evaluated [58]. Ultrasound-guided puncture is recommended to reduce mechanical injury; the necessity of the catheter should be evaluated daily after insertion, and early removal should be pursued [59,60]. Enhanced anticoagulant prophylaxis should be considered for high-risk patients (e.g. those with cancer or a history of VTE) [61]. Timely and effective intervention by healthcare providers for patients at potential high risk of catheter-related venous thrombosis can reduce serious complications caused by catheter-related thrombosis and has significant clinical implications for improving treatment outcomes and patient prognosis [62].

Elevated D-dimer levels are another independent risk factor in the development of venous thromboembolism (VTE) in ICU patients, serving as a critical clinical indicator [63]. D-dimer is a specific end product generated when fibrin monomers, formed under the action of thrombin, are cross-linked and subsequently degraded by plasmin, making it an important molecular marker [64]. Increased levels indicate a secondary hyperfibrinolytic state, suggesting a hypercoagulable condition in the blood. Additionally, elevated D-dimer levels may also reflect endothelial cell damage. When vascular endothelial cells are injured, their surface anticoagulant properties are reduced, making it easier for thrombi to form at the injury site. Higher D-dimer levels indicate a significantly increased risk of venous thrombosis, and D-dimer testing is now routinely used as a first-line assessment tool for patients suspected of having VTE [65,66].

In our study, risk factors with low-quality evidence but statistically significant associations included age, length of hospital stay, and malignancy. Research by Tetsuya Yumoto [6,7,9,10,32,36] indicates that the incidence of VTE increases with age. This is primarily due to reduced vascular elasticity and increased release of procoagulant factors from endothelial cells as people age, which leads to higher plasma viscosity and an elevated risk of thrombosis. Compared to younger individuals, elderly patients with VTE have a higher mortality rate. Studies by Quanhong Chu [1,23,37] have suggested a correlation between VTE risk and length of hospital stay. ICU patients often have severe illnesses and are in a stress state, which causes a hypercoagulable condition. Furthermore, prolonged hospitalization involves repeated vascular interventions and exposure to medications that can damage the endothelium, thereby increasing the risk of VTE. Additionally, VTE occurrence is associated with malignancy because cancer patients often have complex disease conditions and require multiple treatments such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy, which can impair vascular integrity and promote a hypercoagulable state. Moreover, patients with metastatic cancer have a higher incidence of hypercoagulability compared to those without metastasis [67–70].

The results of this meta-analysis provide clear guidance for improving clinical practice: factors with high-quality evidence, such as invasive mechanical ventilation and central venous catheterization, can be directly integrated into the ICU-specific module of existing VTE risk assessment tools (e.g. the Caprini score) [71,72]. Meanwhile, factors with moderate evidence, such as advanced age and malignant tumors, can serve as dynamic warning markers. We recommend a stratified prevention strategy for high-risk patients – those with ≥2 core risk factors [73,74] – considering enhanced pharmacological prevention doses or combined mechanical prevention in addition to basic preventive measures, and establishing an early warning pathway guided by dynamic D-dimer monitoring [75]. This evidence-based, precision prevention system is expected to improve VTE prevention outcomes in ICU patients while ensuring safety.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: A potential limitation of this meta-analysis is the variability in study samples, study designs, patient characteristics and management, as well as the definitions of VTE events. Although meta-analysis offers greater statistical power, many estimates are associated with wide confidence intervals. Due to limitations in clinical data systems, data in this study were manually extracted and double-checked rather than automatically extracted, which may introduce some bias. Even with study registries, it remains challenging to comprehensively obtain studies with negative results. Furthermore, the level of evidence is limited since this is a secondary research; it does not provide as strong support for clinical decision-making as multicenter large-sample randomized controlled trials. Therefore, it has shortcomings in providing evidence for treatment and prevention strategies. Hence, it is necessary to conduct multicenter studies using multivariable models to validate predictors of VTE occurrence. It is indeed important to use systematic review guidelines to formulate recommendations. During this process, it should be kept in mind that statistical heterogeneity may lead to an overestimation or underestimation of the true effect of interventions.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This meta-analysis identified seven significant risk factors associated with VTE occurrence in ICU patients. Among them, invasive mechanical ventilation, central venous catheter, and D-dimer showed higher quality evidence for their correlation with VTE risk. These findings may help identify ICU patients at higher risk of VTE, provide information for developing risk assessment models for patient stratification, and ultimately improve patient outcomes through optimal screening and management strategies.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: All authors contributed to the study conception and design. Xiaoli Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing- Original draft, Data curation, Visualization were performed; Xiaoli Zhou: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Reviewing and Editing were performed; Yuehong Qiu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration were performed. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The original contributions presented in the study are included in the article/supplementary material, further inquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.
